title: The 5 Core Questions Every Playbook Answers

questions.question: What percentage of solo founders have a written sales process?
questions.explanation: Only 20% of solo founders have a written sales process. The other 80% are improvising, which produces inconsistent results and the feast-or-famine revenue cycle that kills more businesses than bad products.

questions.question: What makes a playbook different from a strategy document?

options.text: A playbook is longer and more detailed
A playbook fits on one page and tells you what to do tomorrow morning
A playbook is only for sales teams, not solo founders

questions.explanation: A strategy document describes aspirations. A playbook answers 5 ruthlessly specific questions, fits on one page, and you tape it to your wall. Then you do exactly what the page says, every day, for 90 days.

questions.question: According to the Playbook-First Principle, what happens if your system doesn't fit on one page?
questions.explanation: If it doesn't fit on one page, you lack clarity. Complexity is where execution goes to die. The one-page constraint is a forcing function that strips away everything except what matters.

questions.question: Most solo founders skip building a playbook because they lack the knowledge to do it.
questions.explanation: False. Most skip it not from lack of knowledge but from lack of commitment. They know what they should do but haven't written it down with enough specificity to execute consistently.

questions.question: How clearly documented is your current acquisition system? Rate your consistency with daily/weekly activities on a 1-10 scale. What would change if you had a one-page playbook taped to your wall?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response honestly rates their documentation and consistency, identifies the gap between knowing and doing, and articulates how a specific, written playbook would change their daily execution behavior.
